original_reasoning: اللَّقْلاق and babbler/croaker share a direct noisy vocal sense.
Arabic stingy descriptor and Greek very harsh/difficult meet in a clear severity concept.
Arabic filling by flood or a water-filled place aligns well with the Greek river gloss.

Arabic zawj = pair/couple/yoking together (married couple, pair). Greek Ζεῦγμα = 'joining/yoking' (from ζεύγνυμι 'to yoke'), originally a bridge/place of joining the Euphrates; the toponym's etymology is literally 'yoking'. Arabic zawj and Greek zeugos/zeugma share the conceptual core 'pair/yoke' — this is a well-known Semitic-IE yoking etymon chain (cf. Latin iugum, Sanskrit yuga). The place-name is derived from the very concept matching the Arabic.
Something that befalls or closes in maps well to an afflicted bodily condition.
Arabic settling or becoming fixed aligns well with Greek 'had held' through the shared notion of firm holding or established state.
Arabic 'invoked against his enemy' strongly meets Greek destruction/perishing through the curse-to-ruin chain.
